questions.question: What does 'semi-hard mining' mean in triplet-loss training?
options: Randomly sampling triplets from the training set each epoch
Using only the hardest possible negatives — the batch items closest to the anchor

For each anchor, selecting a negative that is further than the positive but still within the margin; easy negatives already satisfy the loss and contribute no gradient, while the hardest negatives can collapse training — semi-hard is the informative sweet spot
Mining triplets from a separate held-out dataset to prevent overfitting
questions.explanation: Triplet loss gradient is non-zero only when d(a,n) < d(a,p) + margin. Easy negatives (already far) give zero gradient. Very hard negatives (closer than the positive) can produce noisy gradients that collapse the embedding. Semi-hard negatives — further than the positive but within margin — are where the loss is consistently informative. FaceNet introduced this recipe in 2015.

questions.question: In the triplet loss L = max(0, d(a,p)² − d(a,n)² + margin), what does the margin parameter enforce?
options: It enforces a minimum gap between anchor-positive and anchor-negative distances; without a margin, the model could satisfy the loss with d(a,p) = d(a,n) − ε and clusters would never become cleanly separated
It sets the maximum allowed learning rate for negative-pair updates
It is the maximum permitted distance between anchor and positive pairs
It prevents gradient explosion when a very hard negative is encountered
questions.explanation: Without margin, the loss is satisfied as soon as d(a,n) > d(a,p). That is a weak constraint — the anchor and negative could be just 0.001 apart. The margin forces d(a,n) > d(a,p) + margin, creating a clear gap. Typical values are 0.2–1.0. Larger margin → harder constraint → more informative but slower convergence.

questions.question: The triplet_loss function returns F.relu(d_ap − d_an + margin).mean(). What does the relu accomplish?
options: Applies L2 normalisation to the distance values before averaging
Clips each triplet's contribution at a maximum value to prevent training instability
Converts pairwise distances to similarity scores using softmax-equivalent scaling

Clamps the contribution of each triplet to zero when d_an > d_ap + margin — easy triplets where the negative is already sufficiently far contribute no gradient, so only informative triplets drive learning
questions.explanation: F.relu(x) = max(0, x). When d_an − d_ap > margin, the inside expression is negative, relu clamps it to zero, and that triplet contributes no gradient. Only triplets where the negative is too close (inside the margin) produce non-zero loss and drive the encoder to push negatives further away. This is by design — easy triplets should not dominate the gradient.

questions.question: In the recall_at_k function, similarity is computed as sim = query_emb @ gallery_emb.T (inner product). What assumption makes this equivalent to cosine similarity?
options: Gallery embeddings are padded to a fixed length before indexing

The inner product of L2-normalised vectors equals cosine similarity; the Encoder.forward method applies F.normalize(…, dim=-1) to every output, so inner product and cosine are identical — mixing normalised and unnormalised embeddings silently corrupts rankings
Gallery embeddings must be zero-centred relative to the query batch
Batch normalisation is applied to embeddings before the FAISS index is built
questions.explanation: cos(a, b) = (a · b) / (‖a‖ · ‖b‖). If both vectors are L2-normalised (‖a‖ = ‖b‖ = 1), the denominator is 1 and inner product equals cosine. The Encoder class in this lesson applies F.normalize in forward(), ensuring all embeddings entering recall_at_k are unit-norm. Using raw (unnormalised) embeddings with inner product rankings vectors by magnitude, not direction.

questions.question: Your retrieval system reports recall@10 = 0.95 but recall@1 = 0.42. What should you conclude?
options: The evaluation pipeline is broken — the metrics cannot both be correct
Data is leaking from the gallery set into the query set
The model is overfitting to training classes and under-performs on unseen test classes

The embedding space has the right global structure — relevant items are in the top 10 — but ranking within the top 10 is noisy; a re-ranking model (cross-encoder, second-stage scorer) or longer metric-learning fine-tune will improve recall@1 without hurting recall@10
questions.explanation: recall@K vs @1 tells you where the information sits. High recall@10 with low recall@1 means retrieval is approximately right but the model is not confident about ordering the top few candidates. Re-ranking — a separate model that scores every top-K candidate against the query — is the production fix. Search systems like Pinterest Visual Search and Google Lens use exactly this two-stage pattern.

questions.question: Cosine similarity on unnormalised embeddings is not cosine similarity. What goes wrong?
options: Nothing — the formula is correct as long as the same encoder produced both embeddings

It mixes direction with magnitude; cos(a, b) is only correct when both vectors are L2-normalised. On unnormalised embeddings, large-norm vectors dominate rankings regardless of direction, skewing results toward samples the encoder is most 'confident' about rather than most similar
PyTorch raises a shape mismatch error when embeddings are unnormalised
The matrix multiply overflows float32 precision for typical embedding dimensions
questions.explanation: cos(a, b) = (a · b) / (‖a‖ · ‖b‖). Omitting the denominator gives raw inner product, which ranks by direction weighted by magnitude. Across a typical neural encoder, embedding norms vary 2–3× across samples, and that magnitude variation dominates the ranking. Always L2-normalise before computing cosine similarity or before adding vectors to a FAISS IndexFlatIP index.
